text: Darker roasts have higher solubility and extract faster than lighter roasts Depends on: roast_level. Solubility increases with roast darkness; faster extraction suggests coarser grind adjustment
Darker roasts often require a coarser grind to manage faster extraction Depends on: roast_level, solubility. Practical adjustment to compensate for increased solubility in dark roasts
Fresh beans off-gas CO2 after roasting, causing gushing and unstable flow Depends on: days_post_roast. CO2 degassing is most pronounced in the first few days post-roast
Bean origin and processing method (washed vs natural) determine the flavor ceiling Depends on: origin, process_type. Brewing technique can only reveal inherent notes, not create new ones
Beans should rest at least 7 days after roasting before use Depends on: days_post_roast. Values: ≥7 days. Breakpoints: 7 days. Minimum rest period to allow CO2 degassing and achieve stable extraction
Beans should be used within one month of roasting Depends on: days_post_roast. Values: ≤30 days. Breakpoints: 30 days. Upper bound for optimal flavor retention
Optimal espresso window is 7 to 21 days after roasting Depends on: days_post_roast. Values: 7–21 days. Breakpoints: 7 days; 21 days. Before day 7: excessive gassing; after day 21: flavor degradation
Beans before 7 days post-roast are too gassy for optimal espresso Depends on: days_post_roast. Values: <7 days. Breakpoints: 7 days. CO2 degassing causes gushing and unstable flow
Beans after 21 days post-roast show flavor degradation Depends on: days_post_roast. Values: >21 days. Breakpoints: 21 days. Beans go flat; flavor becomes muted
Standard double espresso dose is 18 grams of coffee Values: 18 g. Reference dose for double espresso preparation
Sour shot indicates under-extraction Diagnostic indicator of insufficient soluble compound dissolution
Grinding finer increases extraction yield Depends on: grind size. Increases surface area and flow resistance
Raising water temperature increases extraction yield Depends on: water temperature. Higher temperature accelerates solubility
Bitter shot indicates over-extraction Diagnostic indicator of excessive soluble compound dissolution
Grinding coarser decreases extraction yield Depends on: grind size. Decreases surface area and flow resistance
Lowering water temperature decreases extraction yield Depends on: water temperature. Lower temperature reduces solubility rate

Extraction yield is calculated as TDS × beverage mass ÷ dry dose Depends on: TDS, beverage mass, dry dose. Formula: extraction_yield = (TDS × beverage_mass) / dry_dose. Standard SCA measurement method
Target extraction yield for espresso is 18–22 percent Depends on: extraction yield. Values: 18–22%. Breakpoints: 18%; 22%. SCA standard target range for balanced flavor
Dose is the mass of dry coffee in the basket Fundamental input variable
Higher dose in a fixed basket raises puck density and flow resistance Depends on: dose. Mechanical consequence of increased mass in fixed volume
Brew ratio is beverage mass divided by dose Depends on: beverage mass, dose. Formula: brew_ratio = beverage_mass / dose. Standard metric for espresso strength
Typical modern espresso brew ratio is 1:2 Depends on: brew ratio. Values: 1:2. Contemporary standard starting point
Channeling is water finding a low-resistance path through the puck, causing uneven and lower extraction yield Primary defect in puck preparation
Distributing and tamping level (puck prep) reduces channeling by making flow resistance uniform across the puck Depends on: puck prep. Mitigation technique for uneven extraction
Shot time is an output of grind, dose, and pressure, not a target to dial directly Depends on: grind size, dose, pressure. Conceptual clarification: shot time is a consequence, not a control lever
Chasing a fixed shot time by feel hides the real control levers (grind, dose, pressure) Depends on: shot time. Methodological warning against time-based dialing
Strength (TDS) is driven mainly by brew ratio Depends on: brew ratio. Primary independent axis of espresso control
Flavor balance is driven mainly by extraction yield Depends on: extraction yield. Primary independent axis of espresso control; distinct from strength
Strength (TDS) and flavor balance (extraction yield) are two independent axes often conflated Depends on: TDS, extraction yield. Conceptual framework clarification
A 1:2 brew ratio is the right starting point for most beans Depends on: brew ratio. Values: 1:2. Recommended baseline for dialing in
Scale extraction yield from whatever dose is used, starting from 1:2 brew ratio Depends on: brew ratio, dose, extraction yield. Flexible approach to dose variation
Doubles should run long: over 30 seconds, usually 32 to 38 seconds Depends on: shot time. Values: 32–38 seconds. Breakpoints: 30 seconds. Slower extractions bring out sweetness
Slower espresso extractions bring out sweetness that fast shots miss Depends on: shot time. Flavor consequence of extended contact time
Target brew ratio for a double espresso is 1:2 Depends on: brew ratio. Values: 1:2. Specific application of standard ratio

For a double espresso, 18 grams in should give about 36 grams out at 1:2 ratio Depends on: dose, brew ratio, beverage mass. Values: dose: 18g, beverage mass: 36g. Formula: 36 = 18 × 2. Concrete example of 1:2 brew ratio application

Extraction time for a balanced double espresso is 25 to 30 seconds, measured from the first drop Depends on: shot time. Values: 25–30 seconds. Breakpoints: 25 seconds; 30 seconds. Time window for balanced extraction; differs from Jonas's 32–38 second recommendation
Shot time window for balanced double espresso: 25–30 seconds (Maria) vs. 32–38 seconds (Jonas) Depends on: shot time. CONFLICT: Two independent expert sources disagree on optimal extraction duration
Finer grind size increases extraction yield by raising the coffee surface area exposed to water Depends on: grind_size. Mechanism: increased surface area contact with water
Finer grind size increases flow resistance, which raises brew pressure and slows the shot Depends on: grind_size. Mechanism: particle size reduces void space in puck

Use a 20 gram dose for double espresso, as modern precision baskets are built for it and the bigger bed forgives small distribution errors Depends on: basket_type, espresso_type. Values: 20g. Applies to modern precision baskets; larger bed provides error margin
If the shot is sour, grind finer Depends on: taste_profile. Diagnostic rule: sourness indicates under-extraction

Tamping pressure is overrated; a level, evenly distributed bed is what actually prevents channeling Depends on: bed_distribution, bed_levelness. Prioritizes distribution and levelness over tamping force magnitude

Distribution matters more than tamping pressure; level the bed with a light tap, then tamp with consistent, moderate force Depends on: bed_distribution, bed_levelness, tamp_force. Prescriptive technique: light tap for leveling, then moderate consistent tamp

If the shot tastes sour, grind finer and keep everything else fixed, because the grind is too coarse Depends on: taste_profile, grind_size. Diagnostic rule with isolation principle: adjust only grind when troubleshooting sourness
Pump targets approximately 9 bar of brew pressure at the puck for classic espresso Values: 9 bar. Standard espresso machine specification
Pre-infusion applies low pressure first to wet the puck evenly, reducing channeling before full pressure is applied Technique to improve extraction consistency

Scale buildup from hard water lowers the machine's effective heating capacity and clogs the group, reducing brew pressure over time Depends on: water_hardness, maintenance_frequency. Cumulative degradation effect; threshold depends on water mineral content

Basket size sets the headspace above the puck; excessive headspace allows the puck to expand and channel Depends on: basket_size, puck_height. Relationship between headspace and puck expansion is not quantified

For medium roasts, set the machine to 93–94 degrees Celsius; below 93°C underextraction occurs and the cup tastes hollow Depends on: roast_level, brew_temperature. Values: 93–94 °C. Breakpoints: 93 °C (underextraction threshold). Specific guidance from experienced barista; conflicts with observation 8
For medium roasts, brew temperature should be 92 degrees Celsius Depends on: roast_level, brew_temperature. Values: 92 °C. Conflicts with observation 5 (Jonas: 93–94°C); both sources high confidence
Purge the group head before every shot for temperature stability Routine maintenance practice; aligns with observation 9

Purge the group head for one to two seconds before locking in the portafilter to clear old grounds and stabilize temperature Values: 1–2 seconds. Specific timing guidance; consistent with observation 6

Backflush the group with detergent once a week on a busy bar because rancid oils in the group ruin every shot that passes through it Depends on: usage_volume, maintenance_schedule. Values: weekly. Preventive maintenance for high-volume operation

Clean the group head gasket weekly because old coffee oils are the most common cause of off-flavors people blame on beans Depends on: maintenance_schedule. Values: weekly. Root cause identification: maintenance issue misattributed to bean quality

Milk steaming for espresso involves two sequential phases: air introduction (stretching) followed by heating and folding to build glossy microfoam Describes the mechanical process of steam wand technique

Milk heated above approximately 65°C becomes scalded, causing protein breakdown and destruction of microfoam structure and sweetness Depends on: temperature. Values: 65°C. Breakpoints: 65°C. Critical temperature threshold for milk steaming

Whole milk (higher fat and protein content) produces more stable microfoam and retains it longer than skim milk Depends on: milk fat content, milk protein content. Milk composition directly influences foam stability characteristics

Higher water temperature increases extraction yield, especially of slower-dissolving bitter compounds Values: 90-96°C typical. Breakpoints: 90°C; 96°C. Temperature range specified for espresso brewing
Water hardness increases extraction of flavor compounds Depends on: water hardness (calcium and magnesium). Positive correlation between hardness and flavor extraction

Scale formation occurs above approximately 4 grains per gallon water hardness Depends on: water hardness. Values: 4 grains per gallon threshold. Breakpoints: 4 grains per gallon. Critical threshold for scale formation; represents a hidden dependency affecting machine maintenance
Optimal water hardness for espresso is 50-80 ppm Values: 50-80 ppm. Breakpoints: 50 ppm; 80 ppm. Balances flavor extraction with scaling prevention
Very soft water produces flat espresso Depends on: water hardness. Values: below optimal range. Insufficient mineral content reduces flavor complexity
Very hard water causes boiler scaling Depends on: water hardness. Values: above optimal range. Excessive mineral content leads to scale buildup
Scale formation is the leading single cause of espresso machine failure Depends on: water hardness. Empirical observation from machine maintenance experience
Filtered water should be used to prevent machine scaling Depends on: water hardness. Practical recommendation to mitigate scale formation
